course_title: MRI Pulse Sequence Construction & Image Contrast
course_description:
This course aims to equip students with a comprehensive understanding of Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) sequences commonly used in clinical and research settings. Students will gain an in-depth understanding of the building blocks of MRI sequences, including radiofrequency (RF) pulses, magnetic field gradients, spatial encoding methods, and signal sampling. They will learn their characteristics and manipulation methods and how to combine them to form different sequences that produce images with various contrast weightings, such as proton density, T1-, T2, T2* and other contrasts.

Starting from the basic gradient-echo and spin-echo sequences, students will explore the composition and parameters of various sequences and how to vary them to achieve different contrasts. More advanced and emerging sequences will also be explored, enabling students to confidently manipulate scanner parameters and generate desired MRI contrast.

MR images are prone to artefacts from various sources, and identifying them is essential for MRI researchers and technicians. This course will introduce the different types of static and dynamic artefacts that commonly occur on MR images, explain their causes and the methods to eliminate or reduce them.

The skills and knowledge attained in this course will prepare students of the program for later courses in advanced applications of MRI and practical operation of MRI scanners within the clinical environment.

This foundational course is mandatory for the postgraduate Master of Magnetic Resonance Technology program. It provides essential background knowledge necessary for understanding subsequent courses in the program. It introduces the principles of practical MRI pulse sequence design and construction, applying them to analyse and understand the origin of the MRI signal and its contrast weighting in sequences that are critical for both clinical diagnostic imaging and research imaging.

course_aims: Upon finishing this course, students will gain a comprehensive understanding of the implementation and parameters of various clinically important and commonly used sequences. They will also gain an in-depth understanding of how the choice of sequence parameters affects the contrast of images. Students will be capable of identifying common artefacts on MR images and reducing them by changing the parameters.

learning_outcomes.description: Create MRI sequences by applying knowledge of magnetic field gradients and RF pulses.
Predict the effect of adjusting sequence parameters on image contrast.
Explain the performance of advanced sequences in producing the desired contrast.
Understand the practical implementation and limitations of imaging sequences.
Recognise and explain image artefacts and suggest approaches for their correction.

assessment_details.task_description: Students are required to complete five online quizzes. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Students will submit their answers online. Only one attempt is allowed for each quiz.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
An extension is not available for this quiz due to the answers being discussed in the next available tutorial. However, successful applications for an extension in extenuating circumstances may be offered an alternative assessment. If there are extenuating circumstances why you were unable to complete your quiz, then please email smp.student@uq.edu.au. You will need to do this within 5 calendar days of your quiz and include evidence (e.g., medical certificate).
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Students are required to submit the answers online before the due date. Otherwise, the penalty will be applied. Submissions received after the quiz tutorial session will be awarded zero marks. The tutorial is usually scheduled on the following Monday after the due date, after the lecture.

assessment_details.title: Final Examination
assessment_details.task_description: The final exam will be an in-person, paper-based exam. UQ Examinations will contact students directly to confirm arrangements; the student will either sit the exam at the St Lucia campus (lives within 200km radius of the campus) or will be directed to nominate an exam centre by a certain date. Please ensure you update your semester address in Si-net at the start of the semester. One reminder will be sent and students who fail to nominate an exam centre and will be automatically assigned to the St Lucia campus. Late nominations will not be accepted. A list of examination centres is listed here, but note that Exam centre availability is subject to change at short notice. Students enrolled internally , please refer to the course profile for Internal students for in-person examination arrangements. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Module 1 - Introduction: Pulse Sequence Revision. Module 1 reviews the fundamental concepts of MRI techniques as introduced in MRES7100. Students are required to study the materials on the Blackboard website to familiarise themselves with these concepts.

Module 2 - RF Pulses: Function, Design, Calibration. Module 2 covers the functions of radiofrequency (RF) pulses in MRI sequences. The practical aspects, such as Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) and flip angle calibration, of RF pulses will also be covered.

Module 3 - Pulsed Gradients: Balancing the Equation. Module 3 explains the magnetic field gradients and theirs role in slice selection, and phase and frequency encoding. It will also explore the spatial encoding of MRI signals.
Module 4 - Sequence Construction: Contrast Formation and Spatial Encoding. Module 4 will cover the construction of a conventional gradient-echo and a spin-echo sequence.
Tutorial 1 - Sequence Construction Demonstration. In this tutorial, we will demonstrate the basic process of constructing a sequence.
Module 5 - Parallel Imaging Module 4 covers two parallel imaging techniques: SENSE and GRAPPA. It will also explore their impacts on acquisition time and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).
Tutorial 2 - Quiz 2 Review. In this tutorial, we will review the questions from Quiz 2.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 6 To Week 8 (01 Sep - 21 Sep)
learning_activities.topic: Practical Demonstration: DIXON imaging This one-hour demonstration will showcase the Dixon sequence for fat imaging on a 3T scanner at the Centre for Advanced Imaging (CAI), UQ. The time for the demonstration session will depend on the availability of the scanner and the radiographer, and this will be communicated to the students during the semester. Students are encouraged to observe the demonstration in person at CAI. Recordings of the demonstration will be uploaded to the Blackboard website. Questions for the mid-semester assignment will be based on this demonstration.

Module 6 - 3D Imaging. Multi-echoes: Dixon, MEDIC. This module will cover the difference between 2-D and 3-D imaging sequences, the chemical shift of fat, Dixon's method and multi-echo gradient echo acquisition.

Module 7 - GRE: Steady State - Hahn, DESS, FISP This module will address the establishment of steady state in imaging. It will discuss three gradient echo sequences: Spoiled, Balanced, and Refocused. The module will also explore the formation of both Hahn echo and Stimulated echo.
Tutorial 3 - Quiz 3 Review We will review the questions from Quiz 3 in the tutorial.
Module 8 - GRE: CISS, Feel the VIBE The module examines two 3D fast steady-state gradient echo sequences: VIBE ad CISS.
Module 9 - Turbocharged: MPRAGE or MP2RAGE? This module will discuss three frequently-used imaging sequences: TurboFLASH, MPRAGE and MP2RAGE.
Tutorial 4 - Quiz 4 Review. We will review the questions from Quiz 4 in the tutorial.
In-Semester break Mid-semester break
QLD public holiday - King's Birthday.
Module 10 - FLAIR, DIR. Making HASTE. This module will cover fast spin-echo sequences, including fast spin echo (FSE), HASTE, SPACE and FLAIR. Dual inversion recovery method will also be introduced.

Module 11 - Spotting trouble: Static and Motion Artefacts. This module examines some of the most common static artefacts on MRI images, discussing their origin, manifestation, and potential remedy methods.
Tutorial 5 - Quiz 5 Review. We will review the questions from Quiz 5 in the tutorial.
Course Revision Revision and catch-ups.
